content.title: $\varepsilon$-Neighborhood
content.content:
For $a \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, the **$\varepsilon$-neighborhood** 
 of $a$ is the open interval
\[
V_\varepsilon(a) = (a - \varepsilon, a + \varepsilon) = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : |x - a| < \varepsilon\}.
\]

content.title: Interior Point
content.content:
A point $x$ is an **interior point** of a set 
$S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ if there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that 
$V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq S$.

The **interior** of $S$, denoted $\text{int}(S)$ or $S^\circ$, is the 
set of all interior points of $S$.

content.title: Open Set
content.content:
A set $O \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is **open** if every 
point of $O$ is an interior point of $O$. Equivalently, $O$ is open if 
$O = \text{int}(O)$.

content.title: Open Sets
content.problem:
1. Any open interval $(a, b)$ is an open set.

2. $\mathbb{R}$ is open (every point has a neighborhood in $\mathbb{R}$).

3. $\emptyset$ is open (vacuouslyâ€”there are no points to check).

4. $[0, 1)$ is *not* open: $0$ is not an interior point.

content.title: Properties of Open Sets
content.content:
1. The union of any collection of open sets is open.

2. The intersection of finitely many open sets is open.

3. $\mathbb{R}$ and $\emptyset$ are open.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) Let $\{O_\alpha\}$ be a collection of open sets and $x \in \bigcup O_\alpha$. 
Then $x \in O_\alpha$ for some $\alpha$. Since $O_\alpha$ is open, there 
exists $\varepsilon > 0$ with $V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq O_\alpha \subseteq \bigcup O_\alpha$.

(b) Let $O_1, \ldots, O_n$ be open and $x \in \bigcap_{i=1}^n O_i$. For each 
$i$, there exists $\varepsilon_i > 0$ with $V_{\varepsilon_i}(x) \subseteq O_i$. 
Let $\varepsilon = \min\{\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_n\} > 0$. Then 
$V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq \bigcap O_i$.

Infinite intersections of open sets need not be open. For example, 
$\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1/n, 1/n) = \{0\}$, which is not open.

content.title: Limit Point
content.content:
A point $x$ is a **limit point** (or *cluster point* 
or *accumulation point*) of $S$ if every $\varepsilon$-neighborhood 
of $x$ contains a point of $S$ other than $x$ itself.

content.title: Closed Set
content.content:
A set $F \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is **closed** if it 
contains all its limit points.

content.title: Complement Characterization
content.content:
A set $F$ is closed if and only if its complement $F^c = \mathbb{R} \setminus F$ 
is open.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ Suppose $F$ is closed and $x \in F^c$. Then $x$ is not a 
limit point of $F$ (since all limit points are in $F$). So there exists 
$\varepsilon > 0$ with $V_\varepsilon(x) \cap F = \emptyset$ (or just 
$\{x\}$, but $x \notin F$). Thus $V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq F^c$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose $F^c$ is open and $x$ is a limit point of $F$. If 
$x \notin F$, then $x \in F^c$. Since $F^c$ is open, some 
$V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq F^c$, contradicting that $x$ is a limit point.

content.title: Sequential Characterization of Closed Sets
content.content:
A set $F$ is closed if and only if whenever $(x_n)$ is a sequence in $F$ 
with $x_n \to x$, we have $x \in F$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ If $x_n \to x$ and all $x_n \in F$, then $x$ is a limit 
point of $F$ (or $x = x_n$ for some $n$), so $x \in F$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Let $x$ be a limit point of $F$. For each $n$, the set 
$V_{1/n}(x) \cap (F \setminus \{x\})$ is nonempty; choose $x_n$ from it. 
Then $x_n \to x$ and $x_n \in F$, so by hypothesis $x \in F$.

content.title: Closure
content.content:
The **closure** of a set $S$, denoted $\overline{S}$ or 
$\text{cl}(S)$, is the union of $S$ with its limit points:
\[
\overline{S} = S \cup \{\text{limit points of } S\}.
\]

content.title: Boundary
content.content:
The **boundary** of $S$, denoted $\partial S$, is
\[
\partial S = \overline{S} \cap \overline{S^c}.
\]
